seoTitle: How to Record a Bounced Check in QuickBooks Online
metaDescription: Record a bounced check in QuickBooks Online correctly to keep your cash flow and outstanding payments accurate after reconciliation.
intro: A bounced check needs to be recorded properly so your cash flow and outstanding payment tracking stay accurate. This guide covers how to record a bounced check in QuickBooks Online and reconcile your accounts afterward, useful for small businesses and larger organizations that regularly track incoming payments.

steps.heading: Confirm the check bounced
steps.body: Verify with your bank that the check was returned before making any changes in QuickBooks Online.

steps.heading: Reverse the original payment
steps.body: Undo or adjust the original payment entry so the invoice or transaction reflects that it's still outstanding.

steps.heading: Record any related bank fees
steps.body: If your bank charged a fee for the bounced check, log that as a separate transaction so your expenses stay accurate.

steps.heading: Notify the customer if applicable
steps.body: Reach out to the customer regarding the bounced payment and any next steps for resolving the balance.

steps.heading: Reconcile your accounts
steps.body: After making the adjustments, reconcile your bank account to confirm your records match your actual bank statement.

faq.question: How do I know if a check bounced?
faq.answer: Your bank statement or notification will typically show the check as returned or declined, which should prompt you to update QuickBooks Online.

faq.question: Do I need to record bank fees separately from the bounced check?
faq.answer: Yes, any fees your bank charges related to the bounced check should be recorded as their own transaction for accurate expense tracking.

faq.question: What common mistakes should I avoid?
faq.answer: Forgetting to reverse the original payment is a common mistake that leaves your invoice or transaction incorrectly marked as paid.

faq.question: Does a bounced check affect my financial reports?
faq.answer: Yes, it affects your cash flow and outstanding balances, so it's worth reviewing related reports after recording the reversal.
